text:
Explore (Digging In)

Providing for Individual Needs

Have Learning Aid Template: Blank 100s Grids and Learning Aid Template: Blank Grids and tape Diagrams available for students to use as needed.

Suggested Questions

  • The total in the table is 30 students. To predict the number with a widow's peak for a new total, would a new percent bar help?
• Where would you put the 500 on this percent bar?
500 would be above 100% at the right-hand end of the percent bar because both represent the total.
• You found that the percentage of students with a widow's peak was 60% for the given data. Where would 60% go on your new percent bar?
60% would be at the sixth increment of 10%.

Some students msy use equivalent ratios, rate tables, unit rates, percent bars, or double number lines to answer the Initial Challenge. Watch for additional strategies that be can shared during the Summarize. (portrayal)

What If...? Situation A is important since it connects to the 1% strategy. Be prepares to help students (or even whole class) interpret the percent bar in the What If...?. (Language)

As students work on What If...? Situation A, observe their strategies. There are two ways to determine the equivalent ratios: according to percentage or according to the total. Both strategies are good ones and worth discussing.

Equivalent Ratios: Finding the Students for Every 1%: Some students use the strategy of finding 1%, 5%, 10%, 25%, or some other convenient percentage that has a "friendly" scaling equivalent. In this case, 1% of 30 students is 0.3 students, or as a unit rate, 0.3 students per 1% of the class. Using this thinking, a student can scale up to the number of students to determine the percentage.
